reasoning: The Lean statement faithfully captures Rudin 2.27(b). The ambient space `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)` is the standard Mathlib representation of ℝ^k. The hypothesis `¬ Set.Countable E` correctly encodes "E is uncountable". The set `P = {x | ∀ U ∈ 𝓝 x, ¬ Set.Countable (U ∩ E)}` correctly encodes "the set of condensation points of E": in the metric space ℝ^k the topological neighborhoods `𝓝 x` have the metric balls as a basis, so "every neighborhood U of x has uncountable intersection with E" is logically equivalent to Rudin's definition using ε-balls. Defining P over the whole space (rather than only over E) is appropriate since condensation points need not be in E, and it does not affect the conclusion `Set.Countable (E \ P)`, which exactly matches "at most countably many points of E are not in P" (Mathlib's `Set.Countable` includes finite sets).
